narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your background includes a part-time work commitment, a middle-income family, an undergraduate GPA of 2.40—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 30, lower than the usual 36.70. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 2 is also notably below the average of about 5.7, reflecting early academic challenges. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 49% chance that you would fail it on your first try—a risk high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, well below the dataset average of 5.53; this lower performance in later undergraduate years signaled to the model a persistent struggle that often correlates with bar exam difficulties for students in similar positions. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your plan to attend part-time, a less common choice seen in only about 7% of students; the model views this as a strength, as it may allow more flexibility for balancing studies and work, nudging the prediction toward success compared to full-time peers. Your undergraduate GPA also helped your case, pulling the prediction away from failure despite being below average, likely because it showed some foundational capability amid challenges. Overall, these top three elements—dominated by that Year 3 ranking—tipped the scales against you when stacked against profiles of students who typically pass the bar. Many admitted applicants have stronger decile rankings and full-time focus, placing you outside their successful patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring part-time law programs that value your unique work-life approach. Building a narrative around your resilience as an Asian male from a middle-income background could also shine in re-applications, perhaps with letters highlighting how part-time study aligns with your goals. With targeted improvements, you can shift your profile toward the averages that lead to approvals and bar success.
